aliases: Steamed Chicken Feet with Spring Onion

english: Steamed Chicken Feet with Spring Onion

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for boiled or braised chicken feet; closest_parent 'chicken food product' (FOODON_03302050) is used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific term for chicken feet; A00LC (Chicken meat and offal) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept specifically covers boiled chicken feet as a prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled chicken feet consists of approximately 4 to 5 pieces, weighing around 150 grams, often served as a main dish or appetiser.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken feet, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 05358)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy data for cooked chicken feet used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent plain boiled/cooked chicken feet without heavy seasoning. Sodium value is adjusted upward to reflect typical soy sauce and salt seasoning used in the Singaporean/Vietnamese preparation style, estimated at approximately 350 mg per 100 g. All other macros are taken directly from USDA cooked chicken feet data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for boiled chicken feet. Chicken feet are composed predominantly of protein, collagen, fat, and negligible carbohydrate (0.2 g per 100 g). By analogy with other high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate cooked poultry products (GI approximately 0–5 in peer-reviewed GI tables), a GI of 5 is assigned. This is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database convention for protein-dominant, near-zero-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (5 × 0.3 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.015, rounded to 0.2 for practical reporting.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and salt seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with boiled chicken feet prepared with soy sauce, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 350 mg per 100 g can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium limits; requesting reduced-sodium seasoning or limiting portion size to one to two pieces is advisable.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled chicken feet contain approximately 0.2 g of carbohydrate per 100 g, resulting in a very low glycemic load, making them a suitable protein-rich option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium-rich sauces are used in moderation.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled chicken feet contain approximately 84 mg of cholesterol and 3.8 g of saturated fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption, and prefer preparations with minimal added oil or sodium-rich sauces.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled Chicken Feet, known in Vietnamese as Chân Gà Hấp Hành (Chan Ga Hap Hanh), is a dish consisting of chicken feet that are cleaned, boiled or steamed, and typically seasoned with spring onion, ginger, soy sauce, sesame oil, and salt. The dish is popular across Vietnamese and Singaporean cuisine and is commonly served as a main course or appetiser. Chicken feet are composed primarily of skin, tendons, cartilage, and collagen-rich connective tissue, with relatively little muscle meat. This gives the dish a gelatinous, chewy texture that is prized in many East and Southeast Asian culinary traditions. The collagen content is high, making the dish a source of gelatin-forming proteins. Nutritionally, boiled chicken feet provide approximately 215 kcal per 100 g, with 19.4 g of protein and 14.6 g of fat, and negligible carbohydrate at 0.2 g per 100 g. The very low carbohydrate content results in a low glycemic index and glycemic load, making this dish suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. However, the sodium content from seasoning (approximately 350 mg per 100 g) and the cholesterol content (approximately 84 mg per 100 g) warrant moderation for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free if tamari is substituted for soy sauce, and is dairy-free. It is commonly found in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore, including establishments such as Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked chicken feet, which is an approved secondary global source. No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry was identified for this specific Vietnamese-style boiled chicken feet preparation. Sodium is estimated to reflect typical seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate based on the near-zero carbohydrate content of the dish, consistent with peer-reviewed GI table conventions. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken feet. 2. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken feet. 3. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken feet. 4. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken feet. 5. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken feet. 6. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is confirmed zero for plain boiled chicken feet; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 7. Sugar (sugar_g) is confirmed zero for plain boiled chicken feet with no added sugar in the preparation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 8. Dietary fibre (dietary_fibre_g) is confirmed zero as chicken feet contain no plant-based fibre; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 9. Vitamin C (vitamin_c_mg) is confirmed zero as chicken feet contain no meaningful vitamin C; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 10. Sodium is estimated at 350 mg per 100 g to reflect typical soy sauce and salt seasoning used in Vietnamese-style preparation; the USDA base value for plain cooked chicken feet is lower (approximately 67 mg per 100 g). 11. The dish name 'Chan Ga Hap Hanh' is the romanized Vietnamese name; the Vietnamese script form is 'Chân Gà Hấp Hành', meaning 'Steamed Chicken Feet with Spring Onion'. 12. The food is served at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant on Circular Road, Singapore, as recorded in the GrabFood Singapore source data. 13. GI is set to 5 as an analogous estimate for a near-zero-carbohydrate, protein-dominant cooked poultry product; gi_category is low per convention.
